articles.title: Massage for Lower Back Pain: What Actually Works
articles.h1: Massage for Lower Back Pain: What Actually Works
articles.meta_description: What the research says about massage for chronic low back pain — which modalities help, how often, and when to see a physician instead of a therapist.
articles.excerpt: Eighty percent of American adults will experience meaningful low back pain at some point. Here's what massage can actually do about it — and where its limits are.
articles.tags: lower back pain, recovery, deep tissue, chronic pain
articles.content_html:
Lower back pain is one of the most universal complaints in the United States. The National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke estimates that around 80% of adults will experience meaningful low back pain at some point in their lives. The question, for most of them, is what to do about it.

Massage is part of the answer for many. It's not part of the answer for everyone, and it's not the whole answer for anyone. Below, what the research actually says, what kind of bodywork tends to help, and the specific situations where a therapist's table is the wrong place to be.

What's actually causing the pain

Low back pain comes from many sources, and the right intervention depends on which one you're dealing with. The big buckets:

  • Myofascial / muscular. Tight or chronically overused muscles in the lumbar region, glutes, hip flexors and hamstrings. Most common, most responsive to massage.
  • Postural / mechanical. Long sitting, poor desk setup, forward head posture pulling the chain. Massage helps; permanent fix requires adjusting the input.
  • Disc-related. Bulging or herniated discs, sometimes with radicular pain (sciatica). Massage can help adjacent muscle tension but doesn't fix the disc; needs medical evaluation.
  • Stenosis or arthritis. Structural narrowing or joint changes, often with age. Massage offers symptomatic relief, not structural change.
  • Sacroiliac (SI) joint dysfunction. Pain at the very bottom of the back, sometimes one-sided. Massage of surrounding muscle helps; the joint itself may need physical therapy or chiropractic.
  • Inflammation, infection, or fracture. These need medical attention, not bodywork.

What the evidence says

The Cochrane Collaboration, which conducts the most rigorous systematic reviews in medicine, has examined massage for low back pain across multiple studies. Their 2015 review concluded that massage may provide short-term improvements in pain and function for chronic (lasting more than three months) low back pain, particularly when combined with stretching and education.

The improvements are real but moderate. Massage isn't a cure; it's an effective tool in a toolkit that should also include movement, sleep, and — for chronic cases — physical therapy or medical evaluation.

For acute low back pain (less than four weeks), evidence is weaker. The body often resolves acute pain on its own; gentle massage may speed comfort but isn't dramatically better than rest, walking, and time.

Best modalities for low back pain

Deep tissue massage is the most-requested modality for chronic muscular back pain, and rightly so. The work focuses on lumbar paraspinals, quadratus lumborum (the muscle most people don't know they have until it's tight), glutes, and hip flexors. Slow, sustained pressure on these structures over multiple sessions can shift patterns that have been there for months.

Myofascial release uses sustained, low-pressure stretches into the connective tissue surrounding muscle. Particularly useful when the lower back feels "stuck" rather than just sore.

Sports massage overlaps heavily with deep tissue but emphasizes movement-restoration: how the muscles around the back actually function during walking, running, lifting. Good for active people whose back pain is movement-related.

Hot stone can be a useful entry point if your back is too tight or sore to tolerate deep work directly. The heat softens tissue enough to allow deeper work later in the session or at the next visit.

Swedish alone is generally not enough for established chronic back pain. It's calming and pleasant but rarely produces lasting change in tight tissue.

Realistic expectations

For most chronic low back pain that responds to massage at all, here's what to expect:

  • Session 1: Exploratory. Therapist learns your body, you learn what pressure you can take. You may leave feeling looser; the change rarely holds more than a couple of days.
  • Sessions 2-3: Real work begins. Improvement starts to last longer. Mild soreness for a day after is normal.
  • Sessions 4-6: If it's going to work, you'll know by now. Sleep often improves. Days without pain become longer.
  • Beyond: Maintenance phase. Most people taper to monthly or bi-weekly sessions to hold gains.

If after six sessions you've seen no meaningful change, massage is not the right tool for what you have. See a physician.

What to do between sessions

The work in the room is half of it. The other half is what happens between visits:

  • Walk. Daily, even briefly. The lumbar spine is designed for movement; sitting starves it.
  • Hip flexor stretching. Tight hip flexors pull the lumbar spine into excessive curvature. Five minutes a day moves the needle.
  • Glute strengthening. Weak glutes overload the lower back. Bridge exercises and clamshells are the best entry points.
  • Sleep position. If you're a back sleeper, a pillow under the knees helps. If you're a side sleeper, one between the knees.
  • Heat at home. Heating pad for 20 minutes before bed reinforces what the massage did.

When to skip massage and see a physician

The following are red flags that warrant medical evaluation, not bodywork:

  • Pain that radiates down one or both legs, especially below the knee.
  • Numbness, tingling, or weakness in the legs.
  • Loss of bladder or bowel control (this is a medical emergency — go to the ER).
  • Sudden severe pain after a fall, accident, or lifting injury.
  • Fever or unexplained weight loss alongside the back pain.
  • Pain that wakes you at night and isn't related to position.
  • History of cancer with new back pain.

None of these mean massage is harmful — they mean the underlying problem isn't muscular and needs proper diagnosis first.

The honest summary

For chronic muscular low back pain, massage works. It's not magic, and it's not fast, but a course of six to ten sessions over two to three months — combined with the boring fundamentals of walking, sleep, and basic stretching — produces meaningful, durable change for most people.

For acute pain, give it a week of rest and gentle movement before booking. For anything with neurological symptoms or red flags, see a doctor first.

Find a RelaxTouch studio near you →

Frequently asked questions

How soon after a back injury can I get a massage?

For minor strains, generally 48 to 72 hours, after the acute inflammation has settled. For anything more serious — falls, accidents, sudden severe pain — get a medical evaluation first.

Can massage replace physical therapy for chronic back pain?

It can complement it but rarely replaces it. PT addresses movement patterns and strength deficits; massage addresses tissue tension. The best outcomes for chronic cases combine both.

How firm should the pressure be on a sore back?

Firm enough to feel the work, not so firm it makes you tense up. If you're holding your breath, the pressure is too much. Tell the therapist.

Will I be sore the next day?

Possibly, especially after the first deep tissue session. It should feel like the day after a workout — manageable, gone in 48 hours. If it's worse than that, the work was too aggressive; tell your therapist next time.

What about chiropractic instead?

Different tool, different philosophy. Chiropractic targets joint motion; massage targets soft tissue. Many people benefit from both, ideally on different days. Ask your provider what they recommend for your specific situation.

articles.title: Tipping for Massage: A Modern Etiquette Guide
articles.h1: Tipping for Massage: A Modern Etiquette Guide
articles.meta_description: How much to tip for a massage, when to tip in cash vs card, holiday tipping for regular therapists, and the situations where no tip is expected at all.
articles.excerpt: How much to tip, when to tip cash, what changes during the holidays, and the surprisingly common situations where no tip is expected at all.
articles.content_html:
Tipping in the U.S. is famously chaotic, and massage has its own particular conventions. The good news is they're simple once you know them. The honest summary in one sentence: at private studios and chain spas, plan to tip 18-20% on the service price; at medical-clinic massage, no tip is expected; everywhere else, ask the front desk.

The rest is detail.

The standard rate

For massage at a private studio or full-service chain spa, the customary American tip is 18 to 20 percent of the pre-tax service price. This mirrors the convention for haircuts, manicures, and other personal services where tipping is the norm.

On a $230 sixty-minute session, that's $40 to $46. On a $200 thirty-minute session, $36 to $40. Round to a whole number that feels right; nobody is doing the arithmetic on $41.40 versus $42.

Below 15% is read as dissatisfied. Above 25% is generous and noticed. Between those two bookends, you're fine.

The exceptions worth knowing

Medical-clinic massage. If you're getting massage at a physical therapy office, chiropractic clinic, or medical wellness center as part of a treatment plan — particularly when insurance is involved — tipping is generally not expected and sometimes explicitly not allowed. The front desk will tell you.

Hospital and oncology massage. Same idea. The therapist is salaried in a clinical role, and tipping policies often prohibit it. A handwritten thank-you note carries more weight than cash.

Owner-operated studios. If your therapist owns the studio, tipping is a kindness but not strictly expected — they set their own rates. Many owners prefer five-star reviews and word-of-mouth referrals to tips. If you're unsure, just ask.

Mobile / in-home massage. Same as a private studio: 18-20%. Often a bit more if the therapist had to schlep equipment up four flights of stairs.

Cash or card?

Either is fine. Cash is preferred by many therapists for two reasons: it goes directly to them, and it's not subject to credit card processing fees that the studio sometimes deducts before passing tips along.

If you forgot cash, no problem — most studios let you add a tip on the credit card receipt at checkout. Some have envelopes at the front desk for tipping the therapist directly even on a card transaction.

Holiday tipping for regulars

If you see the same therapist regularly — say, monthly for a year or more — the December "holiday tip" is a small American tradition worth knowing about. The convention is something like the cost of one session as a gesture of appreciation.

This isn't a hard rule. It's optional, it's contextual, and many regulars instead bring a small gift, a written card, or simply a notably larger tip on a December session. Therapists notice and remember.

The therapist's actual paycheck

It helps to understand where your tip actually goes. At most chain spas and many private studios, the therapist receives a percentage of the service price (commonly 30-50%) plus tips. The 50% you don't see goes to rent, products, marketing, insurance, and operations. Tips are often most of the therapist's take-home on any given session.

This is why tipping matters more than it does in some service industries. A 20% tip on a $230 session is nearly as much as the therapist earns from the booking itself. It's not rounding error; it's their day.

Special cases

You used a gift card. Tip on the original face value of the service, not the discounted price (if any). The therapist did the same work.

You bought a package or membership. Same logic — tip on the per-session retail value, not the per-session membership rate. If you bought a 10-pack at $200/session that retails for $230, tip on $230.

The session was unsatisfactory. Talk to the front desk before tipping or skipping the tip. Reputable studios will offer a partial refund or a free re-do, and the therapist won't be unfairly penalized for what may have been a mismatch.

The session was free or comped. Tip on what the session would have cost. The therapist did the work.

You had a 90-minute session at a non-standard price. Tip on the full price of the session, not on the per-30-minute rate.

Tipping in conversations and feedback

One small thing therapists really appreciate, more than people realize: specific feedback. "Thank you, the work on my right shoulder really helped" carries information your therapist will use next session. A generic "great, thanks" doesn't.

If you genuinely loved a session, tell the studio. A name-checked review online ("Maria did an incredible job with my neck pain") supports the therapist's career far beyond a single tip.

The TL;DR table

SettingTip
Private studio / chain spa18-20%
Medical clinic / PT officeNone expected
Hospital / oncologyNone (often not allowed)
Owner-operator studioOptional; kind to do
Mobile / in-home20%+
Holiday for regularsCost of one session, optional

Find a RelaxTouch studio near you →

Frequently asked questions

Do I tip on the pre-tax or post-tax price?

Convention is pre-tax, but the difference is small enough that nobody minds either way.

Is it ever okay to skip the tip entirely?

At a private studio, only if the experience was genuinely unsatisfactory — and in that case, talk to the front desk first. At medical and clinical settings, skipping the tip is normal and expected.

Can I tip in advance?You can, and some people do (it's read as setting up a comfortable working relationship). It doesn't change the work, but it's not weird either.

Should I tip more for couples massage?

Tip each therapist 18-20% of their portion of the session. If the couples massage cost $460 split between two therapists, that's roughly $40-45 per therapist.

I always forget. Is there a way to set up automatic tipping?

Most studios will save your card on file with a default tip percentage if you ask. It removes the friction entirely.

articles.title: Sports Massage for Runners: Recovery Without the Injury Risk
articles.meta_description: How sports massage helps runners recover, when to schedule it around training and races, and the common mistakes that turn a session into an injury.
articles.excerpt: Training is breakdown; recovery is build-up. Here's how to use sports massage to support the second half — without making the classic mistakes.
articles.tags: running, sports massage, recovery, performance, athletes
articles.content_html:
Training is breakdown. Recovery is build-up. The fitness gains a runner cares about happen in the build-up phase, not on the run itself, and bodywork is one of the few interventions that meaningfully accelerates that side of the equation.

This is a guide to using sports massage as part of a sustainable running practice — without the common mistakes that turn a recovery session into an injury or a wasted hour.

Why running specifically benefits

Running is repetitive impact. Every mile is roughly 1,500 strides; every stride loads the calves, hamstrings, glutes, hip flexors, and lower back in nearly identical patterns. Tissue adapts, but it also accumulates fatigue and small adhesions that, over weeks, lock in patterns of restriction.

Three common runner-specific issues that respond well to sports massage:

  • Calf tightness. The most universal runner complaint. Chronic tightness in the gastrocnemius and soleus often pulls into the Achilles and contributes to plantar fasciitis if left alone.
  • Hip flexor restriction. Sitting jobs plus running creates uniquely tight hip flexors. Tight hip flexors yank on the lumbar spine and inhibit glute firing — both of which set up injury cascades.
  • IT band trouble. The IT band itself doesn't get massaged; it's a tendon. But the muscles that attach to it (tensor fasciae latae, glute med, vastus lateralis) absolutely do, and that's where the restriction usually lives.

What sports massage actually does

Sports massage isn't one technique. It's a category that combines deep tissue, trigger-point work, myofascial release, and sometimes assisted stretching, all delivered with a runner's body in mind.

A skilled sports therapist will spend most of the session on the chain that does the work: feet, calves, hamstrings, glutes, hip flexors, lower back. Less time on shoulders and neck unless you specifically need it. The work is firmer than Swedish but more targeted than generic deep tissue.

The two big effects, both well-supported:

  1. Reduced muscle tension. Tight tissue restricts range of motion; restricted range of motion changes mechanics; changed mechanics overload other tissues. Restoring tension to baseline is high-leverage.
  2. Improved local circulation. Massage produces measurable increases in local blood flow during and after the session, which supports the body's own recovery processes.

What it doesn't do, despite popular framing: it doesn't "flush lactic acid." That's a myth from the 1980s; lactate is metabolized within minutes of the run ending. Massage helps via tissue and nervous-system mechanisms, not via metabolic clearance.

Pre-race vs post-race vs maintenance

Different timing, different intent. Don't confuse them.

Maintenance massage is the bread and butter. Every two to three weeks during training. The goal is to keep tissue from accumulating chronic restriction over the cycle. Work can be moderately deep; a day or two of mild soreness is fine.

Pre-race massage is the trickiest to get right. The conventional wisdom — and what most experienced runners practice — is to avoid deep tissue work in the 72 hours before a race. The rationale is real: deep tissue can produce a few days of micro-soreness as tissue recovers, and you don't want that on race morning.

What works pre-race: a light, flowing 30-minute Swedish session 2-3 days out. Just enough to relax the nervous system and increase circulation, without provoking any inflammatory response.

Post-race massage is similarly cautious. Skip deep work in the first 48 hours; your body is already in a stressed state, and aggressive work can extend rather than shorten recovery. A light Swedish session 24-48 hours after the race, then deep work a week later, is the conventional pattern.

Frequency by mileage

Weekly mileageSuggested cadence
Up to 20 miles/weekOnce a month is plenty
20-40 miles/weekEvery 2-3 weeks
40-60 miles/weekWeekly to bi-weekly
60+ miles/week (marathon training)Weekly during peak weeks, scaled back during taper

Adjust by feel. A weekly massage during a 30-mile training week isn't excessive if your body is asking for it.

Common mistakes

Deep tissue too close to a race. Already covered. Don't.

No cooldown after the session. Walk for ten minutes after a sports massage. Hydrate. Don't go directly from the table to a hard workout (that defeats the point).

Going too hard. "No pain, no gain" in massage gets you tissue irritation, not gains. Pressure should always feel earned, never endured.

Treating massage as a substitute for the basics. Sleep, water, walking, easy days, easy weeks every fourth week. Massage supports these; it doesn't replace any of them.

Skipping intake. Tell your therapist about your weekly mileage, recent races, current niggles, and your next race date. The session changes meaningfully based on those four pieces of information.

What to do between sessions

Foam rolling is the closest at-home substitute. It's not as good as a skilled therapist, but it's better than nothing, and frequent foam rolling between sessions helps you get more out of each massage.

Specifically: foam roll calves, hamstrings, IT band area (technically the muscles attaching to it), glutes, and quads for two to three minutes each, three or four times a week. After workouts, not before.

Hip flexor stretching daily. The couch stretch (bent knee on a couch, opposite foot forward, lean) is the highest-leverage runner stretch in the world.

The integration

Sports massage works best as one piece of an integrated recovery practice. Sleep is more important. Nutrition matters. Easy days have to actually be easy. Hard days have to be planned.

But within that frame, regular sports massage during a training cycle is one of the most under-used tools in non-elite distance running. Most age-group runners would PR by tightening up these recovery edges before adding more miles.

Find a RelaxTouch studio near you →

Frequently asked questions

How long before a marathon should my last deep tissue session be?

Conventional answer: at least 5-7 days. Some elite athletes go 10. Light Swedish work in the final 72 hours is fine; deep work isn't.

What about percussive massage devices (Theragun, Hypervolt)?

Useful for at-home maintenance; not a substitute for skilled hands. Use them in short bouts (60-90 seconds per area) on tight muscles, not on bony areas, and not just before a hard run.

Should I get massage if I'm injured?

Depends on the injury. Acute strains: wait a few days and get cleared by a sports medicine doc. Chronic overuse stuff (Achilles, plantar fascia, calf tightness): often, yes, but with a therapist who knows what they're doing.

Will sports massage help me run faster?

Indirectly. By keeping tissue healthy enough that you can train consistently, it removes one of the bigger blockers to fitness gains. Massage doesn't make muscles stronger; it lets you train more without breaking down.

Should I stretch before or after the massage?

After. Tissue is more pliable post-session; gentle stretching helps lock in the new range. Pre-session stretching isn't needed.

articles.title: How to Choose a Massage Therapist (and What to Avoid)
articles.h1: How to Choose a Massage Therapist
articles.meta_description: Credentials, specializations, red flags and green flags — a practical guide to finding a massage therapist who's right for your body and your goals.
articles.excerpt: Credentials, specializations, the red flags everyone misses, and the green flags worth paying for. A practical guide to finding the right therapist.
articles.content_html:
A great massage therapist will leave you feeling better than you can usually arrange for yourself in an hour. A mediocre one is a forgettable hour. Knowing what to look for shifts the odds dramatically in your favor.

This is a practical guide to choosing a therapist — what credentials matter, when specialization matters more, the red flags professionals will tell you about privately, and the small green flags that distinguish a working professional from someone who just got a license.

Credentials: what's required, what's signal

In the United States, massage therapy is regulated at the state level. Almost every state requires a license to practice, with these exceptions noted at the time of writing: Kansas, Minnesota, Wyoming and a couple of others without statewide licensure (where local jurisdictions sometimes regulate).

Standard requirements in licensing states include 500-1,000 hours of accredited training, passing a national exam (most commonly the MBLEx — administered by FSMTB), and continuing education to renew. The credential most American therapists carry is LMT (Licensed Massage Therapist). In some states it's MT, RMT, or CMT depending on the regulatory framework.

What this means in practice: any reputable studio in a licensing state will only employ licensed therapists. If a place can't or won't show you a therapist's license number, leave.

Beyond the license, additional certifications signal specialization:

  • Prenatal certification for pregnancy work.
  • Sports massage certification for athletes.
  • Manual lymphatic drainage (MLD) certification for post-surgical or lymphedema work.
  • Oncology massage certification for clients with active or past cancer.
  • Neuromuscular therapy / trigger point therapy for chronic pain conditions.

If your situation is one of these, prioritize specialization over generalists.

Match the therapist to the goal

The most common mistake is assuming all massage therapists are equally suited to all goals. They aren't.

For chronic pain or specific injury: look for a therapist with deep tissue, neuromuscular, or sports massage focus. Years of experience with injury work matters more than years in spa work.

For pregnancy: only see a prenatal-certified therapist after the first trimester. The work is materially different from regular massage.

For relaxation and stress: almost any licensed therapist with a good Swedish technique will serve. The room and the rapport matter as much as the technique.

For athletic recovery: sports massage focus, ideally someone who works with athletes regularly.

For post-surgical edema or lymphedema: manual lymphatic drainage specifically. Most therapists are not trained in this; check.

For active cancer treatment: oncology-certified therapist, in coordination with your oncology team. Ordinary massage is often inappropriate during treatment.

Red flags to walk away from

These are situations where a professional therapist will tell you to leave and find someone else:

  • No proper draping. A licensed professional always uses sheets and only uncovers the area being worked on. If you're ever fully exposed without your consent, that's a serious problem.
  • No intake or health history. A therapist who doesn't ask about medical conditions, medications, allergies or pregnancy is operating below standard. The intake exists for safety; skipping it is a red flag.
  • Inappropriate touch or comments. Massage is therapeutic. If anything ever feels sexual, you're at the wrong place — leave immediately and report it to the state licensing board.
  • Unhygienic conditions. Linens should be visibly clean and changed between clients. Hands washed at the start and end. Tools cleaned. If anything looks off, trust it.
  • Pressure to upsell. Sales-y conversations during intake (push to buy packages, products, longer sessions) are unprofessional and increasingly common at chain spas. A skilled therapist focuses on the work; the front desk handles the business.
  • Won't show credentials. Any reputable studio will provide license info on request.
  • Refuses to adjust pressure or technique when asked. Communication is the basic protocol. A therapist who ignores feedback isn't doing their job.

Green flags worth paying for

The therapists you'll want to keep show some combination of these:

  • Asks specific questions at intake. "How does the right shoulder feel today compared to last time?" tells you they remember.
  • Adjusts pressure proactively. They notice when your body tenses and lighten up before you have to say something.
  • Uses silence well. Not chatty. Speaks when there's information to share, otherwise lets the room be quiet.
  • Recommends frequency honestly. A therapist who says "you don't need to see me every week" when that's true is one to keep.
  • Suggests homework when relevant. Stretches, posture cues, sleep position. Indicates they're thinking about your body beyond the table.
  • Punctual. Sessions start on time, end on time. The full booked length, not 50 minutes called 60.

The first session as audition

Treat your first session with any therapist as a tryout. Pay attention to:

  • How you feel during the session — are you tensing because the pressure is wrong, or relaxing because it's right?
  • Whether they noticed and adjusted to your body's responses.
  • How specific the work felt — generic sweep, or targeted attention to what you mentioned?
  • How you feel the next day — energized, slightly sore in a productive way, or unexpectedly worse?

A great first session has you booking the second before you leave. A mediocre one leaves you neutral. Either is data.

When to switch

If after two or three sessions you're not making meaningful progress on the issue you booked for — and you're being honest in feedback — switch therapists. It's not personal; bodies and styles match in particular ways, and a therapist who isn't matching yours might be perfect for someone else.

Tell the front desk politely; they reassign without taking it personally. Many people find their long-term therapist on the second or third try, not the first.

Questions to ask before booking

It's reasonable to ask any of these when calling a studio for the first time:

  • What modalities does the therapist specialize in?
  • How long has the therapist been licensed?
  • Do they have experience with [your specific issue]?
  • What's the pressure range they typically work in?
  • Are there contraindications I should know about for my health condition?

Studios that answer crisply and professionally are likely the ones you want. Studios that get cagey or push you to the booking are a yellow flag.

Find a RelaxTouch studio near you →

Frequently asked questions

How do I check if a therapist is licensed?

Most state licensing boards have an online lookup tool — search "[your state] massage therapy license verification." You can search by name and see the active license status.

Should I prefer male or female therapists?

It's a personal preference, no medical reason for one over the other. Most studios let you specify when booking. Some people are more comfortable with same-gender therapists, especially first-time guests; that's normal.

What if I want to switch therapists at the same studio?Tell the front desk. They'll reassign without making it awkward. Studios are used to this and prefer you stay rather than leave entirely.

Can I see the therapist's reviews before booking?

Most studios have Google or Yelp reviews where individual therapists are mentioned. Filter for the therapist's name. Take both extremes (one-star and five-star) with some salt; the median review is usually accurate.

Are higher-priced therapists actually better?

Sometimes. Often higher rates correlate with more years, additional specializations, or simply being in higher-demand markets. They don't guarantee better fit. The price-quality correlation is real but loose.

articles.title: Prenatal Massage: A Trimester-by-Trimester Guide
articles.meta_description: What to expect from prenatal massage in each trimester — safe positioning, common benefits, contraindications, and when to coordinate with your OB.
articles.excerpt: Pregnancy reshapes your body weekly. Here's how skilled prenatal massage adapts trimester by trimester — and the situations where you should talk to your OB first.
articles.tags: prenatal, pregnancy, trimester guide, women's health
articles.content_html:
Pregnancy reshapes the body weekly, and the body that was comfortable on a standard massage table at twelve weeks is a different body at thirty-two. Prenatal massage adapts to that. This is a guide to what changes — what's safe, what shifts, what to expect — across the three trimesters.

This article is general information and not medical advice. Discuss massage with your OB or midwife if you have any pregnancy-related concerns or risk factors.

The basics first

Prenatal massage is regular massage adapted to the physiology of pregnancy. The work is delivered by a therapist who has completed specific prenatal training (often a 24- to 40-hour certification on top of standard massage education) and is comfortable with the positioning, modifications, and contraindications unique to pregnant bodies.

Two key adjustments separate prenatal from regular massage:

  1. Positioning. Pregnant clients lie in supported side-lying position with bolsters and pillows, not face-down on the regular table. Specialized prenatal tables with belly cutouts exist; most studios use the side-lying approach because it's more comfortable for most clients.
  2. Pressure and area modifications. Some areas (deep abdominal work, certain pressure points on ankles and hands historically associated with labor induction) are avoided. Pressure overall stays moderate.

First trimester (weeks 1-13)

The first trimester is the most cautious phase. Two-thirds of miscarriages happen in the first trimester, the vast majority for reasons unrelated to massage — but the timing creates an association that makes most therapists conservative.

Practice varies by studio. Common approaches:

  • Wait until 13 weeks. Many studios decline first-trimester massage entirely. This is more about liability than evidence; massage doesn't cause miscarriage.
  • Light Swedish only. Some studios offer first-trimester sessions but limit work to gentle Swedish in side-lying or seated position, avoiding deep work or any abdominal contact.
  • OB clearance first. Some studios require written clearance from your OB or midwife before any prenatal massage.

What benefits clients report in the first trimester: relief from nausea (when the work is gentle and not in supine position), lower stress, better sleep, less back tension. The tradeoff for many studios is the legal and emotional risk; the benefit isn't dramatic enough to overcome that.

Practical advice: if you want first-trimester massage, call ahead and ask the studio's policy. Don't show up at twelve weeks expecting to find out at the table.

Second trimester (weeks 14-27)

The sweet spot. Most studios offer full prenatal services from week 14 onward, and most pregnant clients find the second trimester is when they actually feel like having massage in the first place.

What changes:

  • Side-lying becomes standard. You'll be propped on your left side primarily (better for circulation to the placenta), with bolsters under the right knee, between the arms, and supporting the head.
  • Lower back and hips become the focus. The growing uterus shifts your center of mass forward, increasing lumbar curve and creating real strain. Most prenatal sessions in the second trimester spend significant time here.
  • Glutes and hip flexors. Often locked up from compensatory mechanics. Some of the most welcome work in any prenatal session.
  • Neck and shoulders. Postural changes (and breast tissue changes) pull forward, creating upper-body tension. Standard work, just from a different position.

What's avoided or modified:

  • Deep abdominal massage. Light strokes for circulation may be okay; deep work is not.
  • Direct pressure on the lower legs and ankles is sometimes minimized — historically associated with labor induction, though the evidence is weak.
  • Aromatherapy with essential oils, unless your therapist specifically uses pregnancy-safe blends. Many oils that are fine generally are not recommended during pregnancy.
  • Hot stone, deep tissue at high intensity, and certain other modalities.

Frequency in the second trimester is typically every two to three weeks, increasing to every two weeks as you approach the third trimester.

Third trimester (weeks 28-40)

The third trimester is when prenatal massage often does the most. The body is heavier, sleep is harder, hips are wider, and lower back pain is the most universal complaint.

What the work focuses on:

  • Lower back and sacrum. The lumbar load is at its peak. Sustained, supported work here is the single most welcome thing for many clients.
  • Hips and glutes. The relaxin hormone has loosened the pelvis to prepare for birth, but the surrounding muscles are working overtime to compensate. They want help.
  • Sciatic-pattern pain. Common in the third trimester from baby pressure on nerves; gentle work on the piriformis and surrounding glutes can ease it considerably.
  • Edema in legs and feet. Light, slow strokes toward the heart support fluid return. Manual lymphatic drainage by a trained therapist can be particularly helpful.
  • Sleep support. The parasympathetic effect of a 60-minute prenatal session often produces the best night of sleep of the week.

Some studios offer specific labor-prep sessions in the final two weeks — focused work on the hips, low back, and shoulders to support the body for delivery. Discuss with your therapist and OB.

Frequency in the third trimester is often weekly. The cumulative comfort matters; one session every six weeks doesn't move the needle.

When to skip massage entirely

Talk to your OB or midwife before booking prenatal massage if any of these apply:

  • High-risk pregnancy designation
  • History of preterm labor or current signs of preterm labor
  • Severe pre-eclampsia or pregnancy-induced hypertension
  • Placenta previa or other placental issues
  • Severe edema or sudden swelling (could signal a medical issue)
  • Recent vaginal bleeding
  • Deep vein thrombosis history or current concern
  • Severe gestational diabetes
  • Multiples (twins, triplets) — not always contraindicated, but coordinate with your OB

None of these are absolute bans on massage; they're situations where coordination with your medical team matters.

Postnatal massage

After birth, the body has a different set of needs — recovery from labor, breastfeeding posture strain, sleep deprivation, and the gradual return of pre-pregnancy mechanics. Most studios offer postnatal massage from about six weeks postpartum (after the standard OB postpartum visit), or sooner with OB clearance for vaginal birth.

The work is essentially regular massage with attention to the areas pregnancy stressed: lower back, hips, neck and shoulders, and any C-section scar tissue once it's fully healed.

What to bring up at intake

For the most useful prenatal session, share:

  • Your gestational week
  • Any complications or risk factors
  • What position you sleep in (informs side-lying setup)
  • Where you're holding the most discomfort right now
  • Any medications, supplements or recent symptoms worth noting
  • Whether you've had previous massage during this pregnancy

Your therapist will adapt the session from there.

Frequently asked questions

Is prenatal massage safe?

For most uncomplicated pregnancies after the first trimester, yes — when performed by a prenatal-certified therapist with appropriate positioning. For specific medical conditions, always coordinate with your OB.

Can I lie on my back?

After approximately 20 weeks, supine (back) position is generally avoided for extended periods because the uterus can compress the inferior vena cava, reducing blood flow. Side-lying is the standard. Brief moments of supine for specific work may be fine; sustained supine massage is not.

Will massage induce labor?

The evidence that any standard massage technique induces labor is weak. Some specific acupressure points are theorized to influence labor; trained prenatal therapists know which to avoid in late pregnancy. A relaxing prenatal session is not going to send you into early labor.

Can my partner come with me?

Most studios allow a partner to wait in the lobby. Some have larger rooms that accommodate a partner sitting nearby. Ask when booking.

How is prenatal different in price?

Prenatal sessions are typically priced the same as regular sessions of the same length. A 60-minute prenatal session at RelaxTouch is $230, the same as any other 60-minute modality.
